source: CIS AWS DATABASE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.0.0
title: Ensure Data at Rest and in Transit is Encrypted

input_row.source: CIS AWS DATABASE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.0.0
input_row.title: Ensure Data at Rest and in Transit is Encrypted
input_row.description: Once a user is logged in to their AWS account and has access to their Amazon Keyspaces they are encouraged to choose from the following two options to encrypt their data. Depending on which key they select for encryption at rest would store the data according to their preference. For encryption in transit the user is also encouraged to choose from two options depending on if the data needs to be encrypted during transit.
input_row.rationale: Impact: Prevents any unauthorized user from accessing the database and provides security when transferring the data from one location to another.
input_row.audit: 1. Sign in to the AWS Management Console • Sign in to the AWS Management Console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/ with your AWS account credentials. 2. Open the Amazon Keyspaces Console • Navigate to the service using the Find Services search bar or by directly accessing the console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/keyspaces/. 3. Select the Keyspace • Choose the Keyspace (database) for which you want to enable encryption at rest and in transit. • Click on the Keyspace name to access its details page. 4. Enable Encryption at Rest • In the Keyspace details page, click on the Configuration tab. • Under the Encryption section, locate the "Encryption at Rest" option. • Click on Edit. • Select the desired encryption setting:  o Default Encryption: Choose this option to use the default AWS-managed key for encryption at rest. Amazon Keyspaces automatically encrypts your data using this default key. o Customer Managed Key (CMK): Choose this option if you want to use your own AWS Key Management Service (KMS) customer-managed key for encryption. Select the appropriate CMK from the dropdown menu. • Click "Save" to enable encryption at rest for the Keyspace. 5. Enable Encryption in Transit • In the Keyspace details page, click on the Configuration tab. • Under the Encryption section, locate the "Encryption in Transit" option. • Click on Edit. • Select the desired encryption setting: o Encryption in Transit Enabled: Choose this option to enable encryption in transit for data transmitted between your client applications and Amazon Keyspaces. Keyspaces support Transport Layer Security (TLS) encryption for secure communication. o Encryption in Transit Disabled: Choose this option if you do not require encryption in transit. • Click "Save" to enable encryption in transit for the Keyspace. 6. Verify Encryption Status • Wait a few minutes for the changes to propagate and the encryption settings to take effect. • Refresh the Keyspace details page to see the updated encryption status. • Verify that encryption at rest and in transit are enabled for the Keyspace.
input_row.impact: Prevents any unauthorized user from accessing the database and provides security when transferring the data from one location to another.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**

- The control centers around ensuring encryption at both rest and in transit for Amazon Keyspaces, which is an AWS managed service.
- Encryption settings are standard properties of AWS services that can typically be queried via AWS APIs, making them suitable for automated checks.
- The AWS Key Management Service (KMS) and the Amazon Keyspaces service have APIs that expose configuration details about encryption settings, both at rest and in transit.
- AWS CLI and boto3 SDK commands are available to validate encryption configurations for AWS managed services, including Keyspaces.

**PROGRAM NAME:** aws_keyspaces_keyspace_encryption_verification

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**

- **AWS API calls or boto3 SDK methods to use:**
  - Use `boto3.client('keyspaces')` or equivalent AWS CLI commands.
  - Check the encryption settings for each keyspace using available methods like `describe_keyspace`.
  
- **Specific resources to enumerate:**
  - List all Keyspaces within the AWS account.
  - Enumerate each Keyspace to check their encryption settings.

- **Configuration properties/settings to validate:**
  - Verify that "Encryption at Rest" is enabled with either default AWS-managed keys or customer-managed keys (CMK) through KMS.
  - Check "Encryption in Transit" is enabled, confirming that TLS is being used to secure data transmission.

- **Pass/Fail criteria with expected values:**
  - **Pass:** 
    - Encryption at rest is either using AWS-managed keys or customer-managed keys.
    - Encryption in transit is enabled using TLS.
  - **Fail:** 
    - Encryption at rest is not enabled, or uses no encryption option.
    - Encryption in transit is disabled.

- The automation will log results for each Keyspace, providing a compliance status overview for protection policies in place. 

This automated approach leverages AWS APIs to automatically verify encryption configurations for Amazon Keyspaces, ensuring adherence to the specified CIS compliance benchmark.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE

- The classification of the audit approach as Automated is correct. Encryption settings for AWS services like Amazon Keyspaces can be programmatically checked using AWS APIs, boto3, and AWS CLI, making automation feasible.
- The use of AWS SDK capabilities and CLI commands is appropriate for this type of compliance check. AWS Config could also be used to monitor these settings continuously.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 7

- The automation details are mostly correct, but there are some areas that need clarification:
  - The `boto3.client('keyspaces')` is the correct client to use, but the method `describe_keyspace` does not exist. Instead, you would use `list_keyspaces` to enumerate keyspaces and `get_keyspace` to get details about each keyspace.
  - The description should explicitly mention checking for encryption settings within the response of `get_keyspace` or equivalent methods.
- The program name `aws_keyspaces_keyspace_encryption_verification` is somewhat descriptive but could be more specific to indicate both "at rest" and "in transit" checks.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Technical Details:**
  - Clarify the specific API calls or methods to use for checking encryption settings. For example, `get_keyspace` should be used to retrieve keyspace details.
  - Ensure the automation script checks both encryption at rest and in transit separately and logs specific details about each.
  
- **Program Name:**
  - A more descriptive program name could be `aws_keyspaces_encryption_at_rest_and_in_transit_check` to clearly indicate the dual focus on encryption at rest and in transit.

- **Additional Considerations:**
  - Consider using AWS Config rules to continuously monitor encryption settings for compliance.
  - Ensure that the script handles pagination if the account has a large number of keyspaces.

**CONFIDENCE:** MEDIUM

- While the overall approach is sound, the lack of specific method details and the incorrect method reference slightly reduce confidence. However, the general direction and understanding of AWS capabilities are correct.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**

- **Modify:**
  - Update the technical details to specify the correct boto3 methods (`list_keyspaces` and `get_keyspace`) and ensure the script checks both encryption at rest and in transit settings.
  - Consider renaming the program to `aws_keyspaces_encryption_at_rest_and_in_transit_check` for clarity.
- The approach remains Automated, but the technical implementation details need refinement for accuracy and completeness.

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**  
Both AI opinions correctly identify that Amazon Keyspaces encryption settings (at rest and in transit) are exposed via AWS APIs and can be programmatically checked. The AWS boto3 SDK provides the `keyspaces` client, and the `get_keyspace` method returns encryption details for each keyspace. Encryption in transit for Keyspaces is always enforced via TLS, which can be confirmed via documentation and API properties. Therefore, all required audit checks can be fully automated.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:**  
aws_keyspaces_keyspace_encryption_at_rest_and_in_transit_check

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- AWS Resource/Service: Amazon Keyspaces (Cassandra)
- API/CLI Method: 
  - List keyspaces: `boto3.client('keyspaces').list_keyspaces()`
  - Get keyspace details: `boto3.client('keyspaces').get_keyspace(KeyspaceName=...)`
- Properties to Check:
  - For encryption at rest: In the `get_keyspace` response, check the `encryptionSpecification` block:
    - `encryptionType` should be `AWS_OWNED_KMS_KEY` or `CUSTOMER_MANAGED_KMS_KEY`
  - For encryption in transit: Amazon Keyspaces enforces TLS 1.2+ for all connections (cannot be disabled), but the script should confirm this via documentation reference or API property if exposed.
- Pass Criteria:
  - Every keyspace has `encryptionSpecification.encryptionType` set to `AWS_OWNED_KMS_KEY` or `CUSTOMER_MANAGED_KMS_KEY`
  - (Optional) Confirm that no keyspace is missing the `encryptionSpecification` block.
  - (Informational) Note that encryption in transit is always enabled for Keyspaces.
- Fail Criteria:
  - Any keyspace missing `encryptionSpecification` or with `encryptionType` not set to one of the above values.
  - (Informational) If documentation/API ever exposes a way to disable TLS, flag if not enabled.

---

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**  
Both AI opinions agreed on automation. The second opinion correctly clarified the exact boto3 methods (`get_keyspace` instead of `describe_keyspace`) and recommended a more descriptive program name, which has been incorporated.

**SOURCE:** CIS AWS DATABASE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.0.0
